title: The kākāpō
content:
The kākāpō

The kākāpō is a nocturnal, flightless parrot that is critically endangered and one of New Zealand's unique treasures.

A. The kākāpō, also known as the owl parrot, is a large, forest-dwelling bird, with a pale owl-like face. Up to 64 cm in length, it has predominantly yellow-green feathers, forward-facing eyes, a large grey beak, large blue feet, and relatively short wings and tail. It is the world's only flightless parrot, and is also possibly one of the world's longest-living birds, with a reported lifespan of up to 100 years.

B. Kākāpō are solitary birds and tend to occupy the same home range for many years. They forage on the ground and climb high into trees. Males play no part in incubation or chick-rearing. The 1–4 eggs are laid in soil hollows or natural cavities.

C. The early Polynesian settlers of New Zealand hunted kākāpō and ate its meat, using its feathers for clothing. The fall in kākāpō numbers was accelerated by European colonisation. By the late 20th century, the kākāpō was considered functionally extinct.

questions.questionText: There are other parrots that share the kākāpō's inability to fly.
questions.explanation: Bài đọc nói rõ 'It is the world's only flightless parrot' → chỉ có mình kākāpō không bay được → FALSE.

questions.questionText: The kākāpō can live for a century.
questions.explanation: Bài đọc đề cập 'lifespan of up to 100 years' → TRUE.

questions.questionText: Adult male kākāpō bring food back to nesting females.
questions.explanation: 'Males play no part in incubation or chick-rearing' → FALSE.

questions.questionText: What was the main food source used by early Polynesian settlers from the kākāpō?
questions.explanation: Bài đề cập 'The early settlers... ate its meat' → C.

questions.questionText: The kākāpō nests are created in ___ where eggs are laid.
questions.explanation: 'The 1–4 eggs are laid in soil hollows' → soil.

questions.questionText: The arrival of ___ accelerated the fall in kākāpō numbers.
questions.explanation: Bài đọc đề cập 'The fall in kākāpō numbers was accelerated by European colonisation.'

title: The History of Glass
content:
The History of Glass

From its accidental discovery thousands of years ago to its central role in modern technology, glass has shaped human civilisation in profound ways.

A. Archaeological evidence suggests that the first glass objects, such as beads and vessels, were manufactured in ancient Mesopotamia and Egypt around 3500 BC. Early glassmakers discovered that heating a mixture of sand, soda ash, and limestone produced a material that could be shaped and, once cooled, was transparent and hard.

B. The invention of glassblowing around 50 BC revolutionised glass production. By gathering molten glass on the end of a hollow iron pipe and blowing air through it, craftsmen could rapidly produce thin-walled vessels of various shapes. This technique spread throughout the Roman Empire and drastically reduced the cost of glass objects.

C. In the 13th century, Venetian glassmakers on the island of Murano developed cristallo — a nearly colourless, highly transparent glass. The Venetian glassmakers jealously guarded their secrets; leaving the island without permission was punishable by death. Nevertheless, Venetian techniques gradually spread across Europe.

D. The industrial revolution brought mechanised glass production. By the early 20th century, machines could produce flat sheet glass continuously, making large windows affordable for ordinary households and enabling the construction of glass-fronted buildings.

questions.questionText: The earliest glass objects were discovered by archaeologists in China.
questions.explanation: Bài đọc đề cập 'ancient Mesopotamia and Egypt' → không phải China → FALSE.

questions.questionText: Glassblowing made glass objects quicker to produce.
questions.explanation: 'craftsmen could rapidly produce thin-walled vessels' → TRUE.

questions.questionText: Venetian glassmakers freely shared their techniques with other European countries.
questions.explanation: 'The Venetian glassmakers jealously guarded their secrets' → FALSE.
'The invention of glassblowing around 50 BC' → B.

questions.questionText: Venetian glassmakers developed a glass called ___ which was nearly colourless.
questions.explanation: 'developed cristallo — a nearly colourless, highly transparent glass' → cristallo.

questions.questionText: Sand, soda ash, and ___ were the main ingredients of early glass.
questions.explanation: 'heating a mixture of sand, soda ash, and limestone' → limestone.

title: Urban Farming
content:
Urban Farming

As cities expand and populations grow, urban farming is increasingly seen as a solution to food security, environmental sustainability, and community well-being.

A. Urban farming refers to the practice of growing, processing, and distributing food in or around urban areas. It encompasses a wide range of activities, from rooftop gardens and community allotments to sophisticated vertical farms using artificial lighting and hydroponics — a method of growing plants without soil, using nutrient-rich water instead.

B. Proponents argue that urban farms can significantly reduce food miles — the distance food travels from producer to consumer — thereby cutting greenhouse gas emissions from transportation. Studies in several European cities have shown that locally grown produce can reduce associated transport emissions by up to 50 percent compared with food imported from rural regions.

C. Critics, however, point to the high energy consumption of indoor vertical farms, which rely on artificial lighting and climate control systems. Research published in the journal Nature Food found that lettuce grown in indoor vertical farms can produce up to 70 times more carbon emissions per kilogram than lettuce grown in open fields, largely because of electricity use.

D. Despite these concerns, urban farming initiatives are expanding globally. Singapore, which imports over 90 percent of its food, has set a national target to produce 30 percent of its nutritional needs locally by 2030. Cities including Detroit, Tokyo, and Medellín have launched ambitious urban agriculture programmes targeting food deserts — areas with limited access to affordable fresh food.

questions.questionText: Hydroponics is a method of growing plants that does not require soil.
questions.explanation: 'hydroponics — a method of growing plants without soil' → TRUE.

questions.questionText: Urban farms always result in lower carbon emissions than traditional farming.
questions.explanation: Bài đọc đề cập indoor vertical farms có thể tạo ra nhiều carbon hơn → FALSE.

questions.questionText: Singapore currently produces more than half of its own food.
questions.explanation: 'Singapore, which imports over 90 percent of its food' → FALSE.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, indoor vertical farms produce lettuce with how many times more carbon emissions than open-field farms?
questions.explanation: 'up to 70 times more carbon emissions per kilogram' → C.

questions.questionText: The term used for areas with limited access to affordable fresh food is ___.
questions.explanation: 'food deserts — areas with limited access to affordable fresh food' → food deserts.

questions.questionText: Locally grown produce can reduce transport emissions by up to ___ percent.
questions.explanation: 'reduce associated transport emissions by up to 50 percent' → 50.
